class_schedule.topic: Foundations of Software Quality and Testing
learning_objectives: Understand the role of QA in the software development lifecycle
Identify testing levels: unit, integration, system, acceptance
readings: Myers, The Art of Software Testing, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Software bugs cost the global economy billions annually. Testing is the systematic process of finding defects before users do. This course teaches you to design test strategies, write effective tests, and build quality into the development process.
key_concepts: Verification vs validation: building it right vs building the right thing
Testing levels: unit, integration, system, acceptance (testing pyramid)
Black-box vs white-box testing: testing behavior vs testing structure
Test adequacy criteria: when is testing sufficient?
Cost of defects: exponential increase with late discovery
lecture_notes.demonstration: Examine a real bug report from an open-source project, trace it through the code to understand the root cause, then write a regression test that would have caught it
setup_requirements: Python with pytest or Java with JUnit

review.value: Testing can only show the presence of bugs, never their absence; this means testing must be combined with other quality techniques (code review, static analysis, formal methods) for critical systems

A bug found in production costs 10-100x more to fix than one found during coding, due to diagnosis difficulty, deployment overhead, user impact, and reputation damage. Testing shifts defect discovery left (earlier)

class_schedule.topic: Test Design Techniques: Black-Box and White-Box
learning_objectives: Apply equivalence partitioning and boundary value analysis
Design white-box tests using statement, branch, and path coverage
Combine techniques for comprehensive test suites
readings: Ammann & Offutt, Introduction to Software Testing, Ch. 2-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Systematic test design ensures you cover important cases without redundancy. Black-box techniques derive tests from requirements; white-box techniques derive tests from code structure. Professional testers use both.
key_concepts: Equivalence partitioning: dividing inputs into classes that should behave identically
Boundary value analysis: testing at the edges of equivalence classes
Decision tables: systematically covering combinations of conditions
Statement coverage: every line of code executed at least once
Branch coverage: every true/false branch taken at least once
Modified condition/decision coverage (MC/DC): each condition independently affects outcome
lecture_notes.demonstration: Given a function specification, derive test cases using equivalence partitioning and boundary value analysis, then measure branch coverage and add tests to reach 100%

review.value: Most bugs cluster at boundaries: the edges of valid ranges, the first and last elements, empty inputs, single elements, and exactly-at-limit values. Testing one value from each equivalence class plus all boundaries catches the majority of defects

100% branch coverage means every branch was executed, but it does not guarantee correct behavior for all inputs; coverage measures what was executed, not what was verified. Always pair coverage with assertions

learning_objectives: Architect a test automation strategy balancing speed and confidence
Write maintainable automated tests that resist code changes
Integrate tests into CI/CD pipelines for continuous feedback

lecture_notes.introduction: Manual testing does not scale. Test automation runs thousands of tests in minutes, giving developers fast feedback on every code change. But poorly designed automation creates a maintenance burden that slows development instead of protecting it.
key_concepts: Test automation pyramid: many unit, some integration, few E2E
Test maintainability: page object model, test fixtures, avoiding brittle selectors
Test data management: factories, fixtures, database seeding
Flaky tests: causes (timing, order dependence, shared state) and fixes
CI integration: test parallelization, test selection, failure reporting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up a complete test automation suite: pytest unit tests, API integration tests with a test database, and Playwright end-to-end tests, all running in a GitHub Actions CI pipeline
setup_requirements: Python with pytest, requests, and playwright

review.value: A test that sometimes passes and sometimes fails without code changes erodes trust in the entire test suite; teams start ignoring failures, defeating the purpose of automation. Quarantine and fix flaky tests immediately

Each test must be able to run in any order and produce the same result; shared state between tests (global variables, database rows, files) causes order-dependent failures that are extremely difficult to diagnose

learning_objectives: Design and execute load tests to verify performance requirements
Identify performance bottlenecks using profiling and monitoring
Apply basic security testing techniques: SAST, DAST, fuzzing
readings: Molyneaux, The Art of Application Performance Testing, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Functional correctness is necessary but not sufficient. An application that produces correct results but takes 30 seconds to respond, or that leaks user data, is a failed application. Non-functional testing verifies these critical quality attributes.
key_concepts: Load testing: verifying performance under expected and peak load
Stress testing: finding the breaking point beyond capacity
Performance metrics: response time, throughput, error rate, resource utilization
SAST (Static Application Security Testing): analyzing source code for vulnerabilities
DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing): testing running applications for vulnerabilities
Fuzzing: feeding random/malformed inputs to find crashes and security flaws
lecture_notes.demonstration: Use k6 to load test a web API: ramp to 500 concurrent users, identify the throughput ceiling, profile the server to find the bottleneck, then optimize and retest

review.value: Establish performance baselines before optimization; without a baseline, you cannot measure improvement. Run load tests regularly in CI to detect performance regressions early

Integrate SAST into the CI pipeline so security vulnerabilities are detected at commit time, not in production; DAST and penetration testing complement SAST by finding runtime and configuration issues that static analysis misses

learning_objectives: Design a comprehensive test strategy for a software project
Select and interpret quality metrics: defect density, MTTR, code coverage
Build a quality culture through process and tooling
readings: Whittaker, How Google Tests Software, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Testing is not just about writing tests; it is about building a quality culture. A test strategy defines what to test, how much to test, and who tests what. Quality metrics provide visibility into the health of the codebase and the effectiveness of testing.
key_concepts: Test strategy document: scope, approach, resources, schedule, risk areas
Risk-based testing: allocating more effort to higher-risk areas
Quality metrics: defect density, defect escape rate, MTTR, deployment frequency
Code coverage: statement, branch, mutation testing
Quality culture: shared ownership, blameless postmortems, continuous improvement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write a test strategy for a payment processing system: identify risk areas, allocate testing effort, define coverage targets, and establish quality gates for the CI pipeline

review.value: Introduce small changes (mutations) to the code and verify that tests detect them; mutations that survive indicate gaps in the test suite. Mutation testing measures test effectiveness more accurately than code coverage alone

When a metric becomes a target, it ceases to be a good metric; 100% code coverage achieved by writing meaningless assertions is worse than 80% coverage of meaningful tests. Use metrics as indicators, not targets

questions.question: A function calculates shipping cost based on weight (in kg). Rules: weight <= 0 is invalid, 0 < weight <= 5 costs $5, 5 < weight <= 20 costs $10, weight > 20 costs $10 + $0.50 per kg over 20. Design test cases using equivalence partitioning and boundary value analysis.
questions.answer:
Equivalence classes: (1) Invalid: weight <= 0 (2) Tier 1: 0 < weight <= 5 (3) Tier 2: 5 < weight <= 20 (4) Tier 3: weight > 20.

Boundary values and test cases:
- weight = -1: invalid (error/exception)
- weight = 0: boundary, invalid
- weight = 0.01: just above 0, expect $5
- weight = 3: middle of tier 1, expect $5
- weight = 5: upper boundary of tier 1, expect $5
- weight = 5.01: just above 5, expect $10
- weight = 12: middle of tier 2, expect $10
- weight = 20: upper boundary of tier 2, expect $10
- weight = 20.01: just above 20, expect $10.005
- weight = 30: middle of tier 3, expect $15

rubric.key: Boundary values at each class edge
Expected results for each test case

questions.question: Explain the difference between statement coverage and branch coverage. Give an example where 100% statement coverage does not achieve 100% branch coverage.
questions.answer:
Statement coverage requires every line of code to execute at least once. Branch coverage requires every branch (true and false) of every decision point to be taken at least once. Example:

def check(x):
    result = 'default'
    if x > 0:
        result = 'positive'
    return result

Test case: check(5) achieves 100% statement coverage (all three lines execute). But branch coverage is only 50%: the if-branch is taken (true), but the fall-through (false, when x <= 0) is not. Adding check(-1) achieves 100% branch coverage.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of both coverage types
Explains which test case closes the gap

questions.question: A CI pipeline runs 3000 tests taking 45 minutes total. Development team velocity has slowed because developers wait for CI results. Propose three strategies to reduce feedback time while maintaining test quality.
questions.answer: 1. Test parallelization: split the test suite across multiple CI runners/containers; with 6 runners, 45 minutes becomes approximately 8 minutes. Ensure tests are independent and do not share state. 2. Test selection: run only tests affected by the changed files (using dependency analysis or test impact analysis); most changes affect a small subset of tests. Run the full suite on merge to main, not on every commit. 3. Layered feedback: run fast unit tests first (should complete in 1-2 minutes), report those results immediately, then run slower integration and E2E tests. Developers get initial feedback quickly and can start fixing issues while the full suite finishes.

questions.question: Explain what a load test, stress test, and soak test each measure, and give a real-world scenario where each is needed.
questions.answer: Load test: measures system behavior under expected peak load (e.g., 1000 concurrent users). Scenario: verifying an e-commerce site handles Black Friday traffic without degradation. Stress test: finds the breaking point by increasing load beyond expected capacity until the system fails. Scenario: determining how many concurrent video streams a server can handle before response times exceed SLA. Soak test (endurance test): runs the system under sustained moderate load for an extended period (hours/days) to detect memory leaks, connection pool exhaustion, and gradual degradation. Scenario: verifying a banking API does not leak memory over a 72-hour weekend with continuous transaction processing.

rubric.key: Correct definition of all three test types

major_assignments.title: Quality Assurance Strategy and Implementation
major_assignments.description: Design a comprehensive test strategy for a provided web application, then implement the strategy: write unit tests, integration tests, and E2E tests, set up CI with quality gates, perform load testing, and deliver a quality assessment report.
objectives: Design a risk-based test strategy with clear coverage targets
Implement automated tests at unit, integration, and E2E levels
Configure CI pipeline with test automation and quality gates
Conduct load testing and report on non-functional quality
deliverables: Test strategy document (3-4 pages) with risk analysis and coverage targets
Automated test suite: unit, integration, and E2E tests
CI pipeline configuration with quality gates (coverage threshold, no failing tests)
Load test results and performance analysis
Quality assessment report summarizing findings and recommendations

criteria: Risk areas identified with severity and likelihood
Testing effort allocated proportionally to risk
Coverage targets are justified, not arbitrary
Strategy addresses all testing levels
Unit tests cover core business logic with meaningful assertions
Integration tests verify component interactions
E2E tests cover critical user journeys
All tests pass and are not flaky
Pipeline runs all test levels automatically
Quality gates enforce coverage and pass-rate thresholds
Failure reporting is clear and actionable
Pipeline completes in reasonable time
Load test results reported with analysis
Defect density and coverage metrics presented
Recommendations are specific and prioritized
Report is professional and well-structured
Comprehensive risk-based test strategy
Effective multi-level automated test suite
CI pipeline with quality gates and fast feedback
Professional quality assessment with data-driven recommendations

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of testing concepts and techniques

lab_assignments.description: Weekly test design and automation exercises

midterm_examination.description: Written exam on test design and automation

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all QA topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for lectures and mandatory for lab sessions.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of testing approaches is encouraged. All test code and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying test code from other students or external sources constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Python with pytest, a CI platform (GitHub Actions), Docker, and k6 for load testing. Lab computers are available.
